sentences.text: I'm ready as good as I am.
Yeah.
Well--
[INAUDIBLE] OK.
Max Epstein, M-A-X.
And Epstein is E-P-S-T-E-I-N.
Max.
Yes.
February 5th, 1925.
Lodz, Poland.
The best in English alphabet will be L-O-D-Z.
To put it in the proper context, I have to say who my parents were, obviously.
My father came from a family of lumber.
My grandfather had lumber yards in eastern Poland, which is today, of course, White Russia.
And they lived in a countryside, because the-- the way they worked lumber is my grandfather had the lumber yards.
But the forests belonged to the Russian aristocracy, or Polish.
And they would then rent, or whatever they call, the forest to cut it.
And that's how they made their living.
And I think that they were-- that they were in that business probably before my grandfather, because I don't have any knowledge of prior generations.
The only thing I know is about my father.
And my father grew into a lumber family and himself had lumber yards.
My uncles, they were all considered experts in lumber.
I know, for example-- just a slight deviation to indicate what kind of a family they were-- that two of my uncles-- my father was quite prosperous.
And so he moved at the war-- end of the war, World War I-- he moved west.
The division was always that the ones who were either Communist, didn't have any money, would go east.
And the ones who had money, to protect it, would move to a freer country, like Poland, which was at that time free country, at least economically.
And so my father moved east-- west and, therefore, wound up in Lodz.
So here we are why I was born there.

His two brothers, among others, went to Russia and actually became-- I don't-- don't want to make this interview a study of the history, but they became actually some very big shots, commissars, in the National Program for Economic Independence, called the NEP, under Lenin.
And-- and they were experts in forestry.
And shortly thereafter-- and I remember letters coming home, seldom-- that one of them was sent to Siberia and died there.
And one went to Leningrad.
And that was, I presume, the time when Stalin took over.
And then just an irony of times that his son became a big shot under Stalin in forestry.
Anyway, my father was in forestry.
This was his business.
He lived in Lodz because he went there and he had a lumber yard.
First, he had a prosperous life, obviously.
When I was born, he built a home.
Not a separate home in the city, but on top of some of other building.
He had a couple of apartments.
My mother-- so that's who my father was.
My mother was born in Grodno, which is today also White Russia, and at that time was Russia, obviously.
And she was born one-- her name was [?
Grad, ?] family name.
Her father was a prominent citizen of the city who, by the way, did it all by himself.
And it was very clear.
He ran away from home from some small town-- I don't remember the name-- became a baker's apprentice.

And eventually, when I saw him, which was age of close to 80, I remember he was very well educated, spoke excellent-- I don't remember his Polish, but his Russian was excellent and Polish probably too.
And he studied by collecting notebooks that children that went to school would drop on the street.
And he would read them, because he couldn't afford-- became a prominent citizen of his city, raised children and educated them.
And my mother was the youngest.
Of eight.
And eight seemed to have been a very magic number, because my father was also one of eight.
So I presume-- like today we have two.
In those days, they had eight.
Right?
It's just another [INAUDIBLE].

So anyway-- and so my father-- my mother, who was the youngest, moved, of course, to Lodz before she met my father because all of her sisters, except one who died during World War I, moved to Lodz for different reasons, married into.
Lodz was a very industrial city.
It was in commerce.
It was called the Manchester of Eastern Europe, because it was heavily in the textile.
And as you know, Jews, obviously in tailoring, that was part of the economic picture of the Jewish employment or unemployment.
And there were a lot of them unemployed.
And they moved-- so three of her sisters were in Lodz.
So naturally, she gravitated there, too.
And that's where she met my father, who was also from the eastern portion of the country.
My father, I think, lived in-- stayed for a while as a bachelor in one of her sister's homes.
And so I think that's how they met.
And they married in '23.
And I was born in '25.
And we were quite prosperous for a while.
By the time the depression hit, my father was a very bad businessman, in my opinion.
But of course, I am a young punk who shouldn't have expressed an opinion about his father.
But he was a bad businessman.
He never really had a knack for it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think that-- at heart, I think he was a socialist.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he lost everything.
And we became really very, very poor.
By the '35, we lost the apartment.
We were thrown out of the house that he built, or the apartment that he built, because he couldn't pay taxes.
And I went to the most expensive school in the country.
It's a Hebrew high school.
And my father, of course, when I started could afford it.
By the tail end of before World War II, he could hardly afford it.
As a matter of fact, I know, not convinced, I know that he was hungry.
And he still insisted on doing that.
And I remember discussions in the family-- and usually the sisters.
You know, there's a clan.
The sisters were always together and always discussing everybody, you know, like in any other society, I guess.
And so the sisters, I remember one of them-- which I probably never forgave her-- said that, that's OK.
He can go to public school.
It doesn't matter if he doesn't finish.
High school was not obligatory.
In those days, very few people went to high school.
You just got seven, eight years of primary education and you were out in the labor market.
The Hebrew--
That's right.

The Hebrew high school was formed in such a way that even though we had the required division of six years primary school, say, and four years junior and two years-- and then two years, they call it lyceum, final two years.
We had the same division as required by the rules of the country.
But the school was always the same.
You didn't go to one school one place and another.
That was the school.
You started at the age of six.
You went to the kindergarten, same building, same school.
And you continued until you were 18.
So that's how they were in high schools.
Not many of them, private, of course.
Public, there weren't too many.
Not many people went to public high schools.
When you say public school in Poland between the wars, you talk about grammar school.
You talk about primary schools.
That's it.
At the age of 14, you were supposed to be through with schooling.
There was no obligatory, those was no compulsatory-- there is no required education beyond that.
It was same thing was in this country for a while, probably at the same time, as a matter of fact.
So that's about where we were before World War II.
There was a very good school.
I was very fortunate to have a very good teacher.
My father knew what he was talking about.
He wanted me to go there because another set of circumstances was that in Poland, which was still very heavily anti-Semitic, Jews could not go to universities.
And if they did, they couldn't get a job at the university.
So most of my teachers were actually qualified faculty for universities if they were not Jews.
So they, of course, had no choice but to go to a very good school, which paid them probably very well.
And that's where I wound up with-- for example, my homeroom teacher was Philip Friedman who became-- after the war, he survived-- became professor of history at Columbia University.
So this was the caliber of teachers that I was fortunate to have.
And today, teaching myself, I-- I agree that maybe you cannot teach anybody anything.
You can make them only-- we can only show them how to learn.
However, I have to say that when you are a child and you are impressioned, then of course, good teachers can make the difference.
And they did.
I was a B student.
I-- my-- my talents and my inclinations have changed with time, very strangely so, because when I was a child, age of 10, 12, I had difficulty with languages in general.
I wasn't very good at languages.
I was exposed to languages, because my father, besides his lumber yard business, somehow or other got involved in publishing about before World War I and during it.
And so-- so we had a house full of books.
And my mother, whose Polish was much better than my father's-- my father never really learned Polish that well.
My mother did very well.
And by the way, they-- they did not speak to me any other language but Polish, because it was very important to retain the purity of pronunciation.
So that-- but anyway, my mother still read me stories from Russian storybooks.
So I have, obviously, learned at least how to read it and how to understand it.
I don't speak it.
But anyway-- [SIGHS] so-- so I had difficulty in languages.
And from-- and I very good at math.
I-- I had a child prodigy type of counting, because my mother would teach me how to count when I was two years old to make me eat or whatever she wanted to do.
And my most-- the greatest entertainment, the thing I liked most is to count.
And so it became obvious that I will become an engineer.
And 20 years later, I still was left with the same impression that I should be an engineer.
It turned out that the war, exposure to different cultures, traveling-- I developed an excellent ability to learn languages.
And actually, I-- whenever I went to a country or was there, I could acquire the language very quickly.
And I was pretty good at it.
I certainly developed an ability to learn, whether it's English or whether it was Czechish or whether it was-- well, certainly Hebrew, I knew.
But I was pretty good at it.
I can write very well.
And so apparently talents or abilities change, either with age or with circumstances or both.
But anyway, this is how I was scheduled to be an engineer in those days.
I was-- I was not very athletic.
I probably suffered like every other child, since I observe mine, from the peer pressure and cruelty of a mob psychology that exists between children.
No, no.
No, no.
I was in the Jewish-- mind you, one thing that my father made sure, and-- that I was going to a Hebrew high school.

And even though I was very well-versed in Polish literature and in Polish intellectual life and I certainly spoke the language very well-- and there was no question about it, I was certainly saved from any of the cruelties of anti-Semitism.
There is no doubt about it.
I mean, I was aware of it.
And you could hear it or you could see it.
But I personally as a child never suffered from it.
Whenever I went to resorts, it was a lot of populated by Jews.
And when I went to countryside-- when I was at school, it was a Hebrew high school.
So in that sense, I did not suffer.
As a matter of fact, I maybe moved to Highland Park because of that.
You know, Highland Park High School is closed on Yom Kippur and Jewish New Year.
And all the Italians have a free day.
I usually-- again, I was not a party-- I was not running with a group, because I was not too keen on the peer pressures.
But I always developed very close relationships with individuals.
I would have one or two very, very close friends with whom I spent most of the time, go to school, come back, you know.
And how did I make this friendship?
Well, of course, this was obviously entirely accidental and synergistic, whatever circumstances were there.
But indeed, I always had very close friends.

And probably it was one of the reasons how my life evolved during the war is that I was always able to make a close friend, because I was the only child and maybe I was looking for a substitute of a close family.
Tell us what your daily life was like.
What would a typical day have been for you as a child?
Well, I would, say, go to-- until the age of 10, my father refused for me to go alone to school, because he was-- he was an older when he had me.
And he was very, very frightened of my well-being.
And so until-- as long as we had nannies, she would take me.
When we came poorer, he would do it himself.
Eventually, the age of 10, 11, I started going to school myself.
And I would get up in the morning.
And usually the walk would be probably the distance from here to maybe a little bit further than schools here.
This-- you're familiar with the American distances of schools and homes, since they are always local.
And then, I would go to-- that school was not local.
That was a private school, so it was-- so you have to take in account the distances.
But I very seldom take-- took public transportation.
I would go there.
It would be 10, 15 minute walk.
And that--
Well, I would get breakfast.
Now, whether my parents were eating at the same times I did, I don't know.
My father would get up if my mother didn't get up and, you know, prepare for me.
And usually it was chocolate and-- and an egg or something.
Usually I was late.
So, you know, it's the same thing.
I think it transferred to my other family, to my family here, too.
They're always late.
So anyway, and I would go there.
And-- and the classes were different than here.
We had-- we remained in the same room all the time.
Teachers would switch, which one of the things I couldn't understand about American schools.
It looks like it's a traffic scheduling, you know.
I walk all the time.
Every time you go to an American school, you always see children moving.
So how much-- when do they study?
Well, in our case, it was the opposite.
You stayed in one room.
That's what you had.
And you-- we used war uniforms from age 13, because that's what schools required.
And it was stricter, obviously, than it's here.
It was a very varied educational curriculum, a very varied curriculum.
I had even at the age of 10 and 11 and starting at 13, of course, I had Hebrew since age of 6.
Four-- four subjects were in Hebrew.
That was Hebrew language, history-- Jewish history.
There was another world history separately, or Polish history.
And there was religion.
And there was the Bible.
Bible was separate.
Studying the Bible was a subject separate from religion.
And then I had Latin.
And I had English for two years, until the Germans came and then we switched to German.
That was an interesting story.
And I'll get to that, I presume, when we talk about the war itself.
And so I had two years of English before the war started and two years of Latin.
No.
Yeah.
And two years of Latin.
And so it was a rich curriculum.
It was very well done by very good teachers.
Then, I went home.
I don't remember the hours anymore.
And I would usually go with my friend.
I don't remember life when we were in our own apartment, vaguely only.
I remember the times were pretty bad, where we had one room or a room in the kitchen or whatever.

And so I usually went to-- since most of my classmates were very, very rich, I mean, very prosperous, since the school was obviously meant for prosperous people, so I used to go to their home usually.
Vice versa, it did not happen, which was a source of a certain probably feeling of inferiorities.
But then again, a child has opportunity for some many inferiorities.
[LAUGHS] At this point, I look at it, it's hard for me to reflect upon it.
But I suspect that if you asked me to go back to those times, it probably hurt me.
It probably was a problem.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Then, I would go home.
And I would do homework either with my friends--
Probably, most of the time.
I wouldn't go very much to anything that looked dangerous, as far as my father would define it.
And I think he defined everything dangerous.
And so the only-- my mother was crazy about theater and movies.
She just was obsessed with it.
So I would go with her quite a bit.
She would drag me to these things.
And I was very much taken-- well, as I told you, I have a cousin in Poland who is much older than I am who is a very famous producer and director of theater.
And so I don't know it was-- had to do with that.
I suspect not.
My mother just loved it.
But anyway, so-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anyway, so I was-- the entertainment was of that sort.
It didn't go-- yeah, I had my uncle who loved-- the father of the director of Polish National Theater in Poland, that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he loved animal movies.
So he was a good-- always a good sucker for getting me to a movie.
So those are the type of things that I would do.
And that would be the typical day.
Summertimes we would, at the beginning, go to nice resort places, usually in Eastern Poland close to my grandparents' house, to Grodno.
Next to it there was a very famous resort place.
And then we would go to another one.
There is a picture you will show later, one of them.
And then, later on, as became less prosperous and it became more difficult for my father to afford it, my mother would rent a cottage together with her sister and her children.
And they-- we would go there summertime.
But usually we will leave summertime, always, because Lodz was like Chicago, full of soot.
You know, it was a city of chimneys since it was all factories.
So it was advisable to take a child out in the summertime, the country.
And that's what we would do.
Until the very last year, where actually my-- my mother put her foot down and said that this guy is now 14 years old.
He's going to the camp.
And my father, of course, was vehemently opposed that, but I guess he weakened.
And my mother sent me with the high school camp to the mountains of summer 1939.
No.
Our schools were divided.
We didn't have coeducational education on those days.
My high school had two-- two boys' high schools and one girls' high school.
There were three different buildings.
And yes, there were only boys.
There was no--
Just-- just that school.
I may-- I remember correctly now-- see, I'm like trying to look back, since I've got the book of some of the people with some pictures after 50 or so years.
And I think that both of the men's schools had the same camp.
But I will not be held responsible for that.
Well, my father, as I-- I didn't mean to say that he was actually a socialist.
He was a Zionist.
There's no question about it, but of the-- of the more liberal persuasion.
He was not religious.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know if I want to say it on camera or not.
But maybe I'll say it anyway, as controversial it may sound.
We had a very nice neighbor in the place where we still lived when my father-- we lived in the apartment my father built.
And Mr.
Zeidman was a very religious man with the beard, you know, with the whole paraphernalia of religion.
And my father always referred to Mr.
Zeidman as an extremely honest man despite the fact that he's very religious.
And it stayed with me a long time.
Now, I don't think that that was-- he didn't mean that as a general statement.
I'm sure some of his best friends-- but the point is that it kind of implied where I came from and what kind of an attitude there was to extreme religion, at least behavior.
That does not mean that my father was not traditionally-- spoke-- not spoke, because I couldn't understand him.
He spoke a different pronunciation.
But he knew Hebrew perfectly.
And his-- he was never-- we never went to school.
Since they lived in the country, isolated, they brought tutors.
And my understanding from my father was that his Hebrew tutor was the one who tutors the Rothschild.
So I presume that he must have had good teachers.
His Russian was excellent.
Well, Shabbat-- well, first of all remember, I was in a Hebrew high school.
And the-- the environment of the school must translate into home.
So whether my father wanted me or not-- I remember, for example, my father at one point insisted I don't write on Saturday.
He thought that, you know, you're going to a Hebrew high school and that's where you study religion.
Well, but strangely enough he did, you know.
So I don't know what that means.
I don't think he was not sincere.
But it's just that he was trying to give me the Jewish education.
He obviously was doing it.
But he was doing it in more modern form, obviously.
And he was not-- so even that was not that-- he wasn't insisting on it.
And obviously, I did, most of the time.
But remember also, that we're talking about age.
When do you defy your parents is the question of-- of growing up.
When did I-- but when I defied my-- my father, it was in his own image, strangely as it may-- strange as it may be.
My-- we had Passover at home, always.
High Holidays, you're referring to New Year and Yom Kippur.
My father would somehow still need to go to pray.
But he wouldn't pray in the regular-- regular synagogue because he didn't belong to any.
So they would rent-- actually it was the Lodz Philharmonic, which had a-- had a hall where they had performances, theater or mostly orchestra.
They would rent that for the High Holidays.
You know, they do that today, too.
They rent the high school auditorium too.
So they would rent that.
And I don't know who they were.
And they all sell ticket, I guess, or something.
My father would go there.
And then, he would find most of his friends, most of the people of the same persuasion.
But they would come in.
And they would pray.
And he would insist to go.
One event that I remember distinctly, my father never touched me.
He-- my mother would spank me when I was very little, you know.
And my mother was always quoting her father saying that-- my mother said that you are my only child.
But I'm going to raise you as one of eight.
And so she would.
My father would never.
Only time he slapped me on my face was once in my lifetime.
And that's on a Yom Kippur.
My cousin took me to the movies.
And the movie was Mario [INAUDIBLE].
[NON-ENGLISH], was the movie.
And she walked out with me.
And she bought me chocolate.
And I ate it on the street on Yom Kippur.
And my father slapped me.
So that was the only time in my life.
So if this gives you an idea of how you handle religion, tradition, and your-- and your identification, I guess that describes a little bit what he was.
